headline: Article 15A Citizenship Applications: No Published Success Criteria, Documented Multi-Year Waits
context: Article 15A grants federal-government discretion to register minors as citizens. JPN publishes application conditions but no substantive success criteria. SUHAKAM has documented multi-year cases, including one 11-year wait.

cards.big: Article 15A is a discretionary citizenship route used by stateless children. JPN publishes application conditions but no substantive success criteria.
cards.sub: There is no statutory checklist for what makes an application succeed. SUHAKAM has documented multi-year casework, including one 11-year case.

cards.big: Article 15A grants federal-government discretion to register minors as citizens. The transparency question is the substantive criteria.
cards.sub: JPN publishes application conditions, document checklists and fees. No published criteria define what success looks like in substantive terms.

cards.big: The 2024 Constitution (Amendment) Bill addressed the maternal-citizenship gap for overseas-born children. It did not address the broader Article 15A discretion.
cards.sub: A March 2025 Federal Court consent order in Suriani Kempe v Kerajaan Malaysia covered eligible pre-amendment overseas-born cases.

cards.big: SUHAKAM reports that stateless children face lack of identity documents, school-admission delays or denial, and high medical costs or denial of care.
cards.sub: At least one Article 15A case documented by SUHAKAM ran 11 years before resolution.

cards.big: Citizenship granted by undocumented substantive discretion is not citizenship. It is favour. The fix is publishing success criteria, not amending the article.
After the 2024 maternal-citizenship amendment, transparency on Article 15A discretion remains the next reform: published criteria, statutory deadlines, disaggregated outcomes.
